food_subgroup: Sichuan-style braised and poached fish dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish (Shui Zhu Yu). Mapped to broader 'fish dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a poached fish preparation in chili-oil broth.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish. A0CKP (cooked fish) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific Sichuan dish preparation. SNOMED CT does not enumerate regional Chinese fish dishes at this level of specificity.

preparation_method: finishing with scorching hot oil poured over spices

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish is approximately 400 g, comprising fish slices, vegetables (bean sprouts or Napa cabbage), and a generous layer of chili oil and aromatic broth.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, carp, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175117) and Vegetable oil, chili-infused (estimated component); composite adapted for Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish preparation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No approved source contains a direct entry for Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish (Shui Zhu Yu). Nutrient values were derived by adapting USDA FoodData Central data for cooked carp (FDC ID 175117) as the fish base, combined with estimated contributions from chili oil, vegetables, and seasoning components consistent with the dish's known composition. Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against USDA carp and oil data and found broadly consistent; values retained with medium confidence. Sodium is elevated due to doubanjiang and salt. Fat is elevated due to the substantial chili oil layer characteristic of this dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Fish and Vegetable Dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate fish-based dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the dish's macronutrient profile: predominantly protein (fish) and fat (chili oil), with only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g from vegetables and seasoning. This is consistent with GI values reported for low-carbohydrate fish-based dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (35 × 12.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.5 g, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (480 mg per 100 g; approximately 1,920 mg per 400 g serving) from doubanjiang, salt, and seasoning, combined with high fat from chili oil.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish due to its very high sodium content (~1,920 mg per serving), which represents approximately 83% of the WHO-recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; if consumed, the oil and broth should not be ingested.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100 g) and low GI (estimated 35) are favorable for glycemic control, but high caloric density from fat (15.2 g per 100 g) requires portion awareness.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content, making it broadly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat content (~60.8 g per 400 g serving) contributes significant calories and should be accounted for in total daily energy management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (15.2 g per 100 g) predominantly from refined vegetable chili oil, very high sodium (480 mg per 100 g), and large serving size collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish only occasionally and in small portions, given its high sodium (~1,920 mg per serving) and high fat content from chili oil, which may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; the fish component itself provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but these benefits are offset by the oil and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High intake of refined vegetable oils and capsaicin-rich chilies may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution with Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish, as the high chili oil content and nightshade-derived capsaicin may trigger or worsen inflammatory responses in some patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish (水煮鱼, Shuǐzhǔyú, also known as Shui Zhu Yu) is a celebrated classic of Sichuan cuisine originating from Sichuan Province, China. Despite its name, the fish is not boiled in plain water but rather poached in a deeply aromatic, intensely spiced broth and then blanketed under a thick, vibrant layer of chili oil. The dish delivers the hallmark Sichuan 'mala' (麻辣) sensation — a simultaneous numbing from Sichuan peppercorns and fiery heat from dried red chilies. Thinly sliced fresh fish — typically grass carp (草鱼) or snakehead fish — are briefly poached to preserve a silky, tender texture. The fish rests on a bed of blanched bean sprouts or Napa cabbage, which absorb the spiced broth. The final preparation step involves pouring scorching hot oil over a mound of dried chilies, garlic, and Sichuan peppercorns placed on top of the fish, releasing a dramatic burst of aroma. Nutritionally, the dish is high in fat (approximately 15.2 g per 100 g) due to the substantial chili oil, moderate in protein (9.5 g per 100 g) from the fish, and low in carbohydrates (3.2 g per 100 g). Sodium is very high (480 mg per 100 g) from doubanjiang (spicy bean paste) and salt, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 400 g serving is approximately 4.5, indicating minimal glycemic impact. The dish is a popular shared centerpiece at Sichuan restaurant meals and is widely enjoyed across China and internationally.

confidence_reason: No approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish (Shui Zhu Yu). Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked carp and chili oil components. The input AI-generated values were cross-checked against USDA data and found broadly consistent with the dish's known composition. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central (cooked carp, FDC ID 175117, and vegetable oil components) as no approved source contains a direct entry for Szechuan Spicy Boiled Fish. Input AI-generated values were cross-checked and retained where consistent with USDA component data. 2. cholesterol_mg set to -1: detailed cholesterol data for this specific composite dish preparation (fish poached in chili-oil broth with vegetables and doubanjiang) is not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central; component-level estimation was not performed to avoid compounding uncertainty. USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) was consulted and does not provide a direct cholesterol value for this composite preparation. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish (fish poached in chili-oil broth with vegetables) is not reliably available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as a single composite entry; the variable ratio of broth, oil, fish, and vegetables makes a reliable water_g estimate impractical without direct laboratory measurement. USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) was consulted and confirmed no single-entry moisture value exists for this preparation. 4. saturated_fat_g (2.4 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (5.8 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (5.6 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked carp and vegetable/chili oil components; these are approximate. 5. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible for non-hydrogenated vegetable oil and fresh fish preparation. 6. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the dish's low-carbohydrate, high-protein, high-fat macronutrient profile, consistent with low-GI fish-based dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 7. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as (35 × 12.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.5 g. 8. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving (400g)' entries); deduplicated to a single 400 g serving. 9. The input source 'Sichuan Academy of Medical Sciences' (http://www.sams.cn) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence in this output.
